intent: A full-stack AI advisor and triage platform for Digital Will agency. Gives Claude access to email, calendar, tasks, meeting recordings, Google Ads, and Stripe through a unified chat interface. Background pipelines automatically process incoming emails and meeting recordings, extract action items, and present them for human review through tabbed Email and Meeting triage views.

systems.name: Chat Engine
systems.intent: The core intelligence loop that connects users to Claude with tool access. Takes a user message, gives Claude the ability to call 30+ tools across email, calendar, recordings, and ads, then streams the response back.
systems.description: Receives chat requests via SSE, runs a loop of Claude reasoning and tool execution (up to 50 iterations), and streams text, tool calls, and tool results back to the frontend in real-time.
systems.why: The team needed a single place to interact with all their work tools through natural language instead of switching between Gmail, Calendar, Google Ads, and Slack constantly. Instead of learning each tool's interface, they chat with Claude and it handles the tool switching.
systems.who: All team members use this daily. Will uses it most heavily for client management, ad performance review, and email triage. Team members use it for checking schedules, finding meeting notes, and managing tasks.

config.name: ANTHROPIC_API_KEY
config.description: Claude API key for all AI interactions

dependencies: Anthropic Claude API (Sonnet for main, Opus for advisor)
failure_modes: Claude API down: chat returns error, no fallback
Database locked: requests wait up to 5s (busy_timeout), then fail
Tool timeout (300s): tool returns error, Claude sees the error and can try alternative approach
SSE connection drops: client can reconnect and catch up from last saved message
validation: Check that Claude's tool calls match user intent - review chat logs for cases where Claude called the wrong tool or misunderstood the request
Monitor tool call success rates - a spike in tool errors suggests an API change or scope issue
Verify progressive DB saves by killing the server mid-stream and checking that partial responses were saved
Test the 50-iteration limit by asking Claude to do something that requires many steps - it should complete or gracefully stop, not hang
limitations: Tool calls are sequential, not parallel - a complex request touching 5 tools takes 5x as long as it could
Chat history loses tool context between sessions - Claude can see what it said but not what tools it used to get there
The 50-iteration limit means very complex multi-step workflows can get cut off
The 256KB output truncation can lose data from large email searches or transcript fetches
history: Started as a simple Claude chat wrapper to test if conversational tool use was viable for the team
Added Gmail tools first because email was the team's biggest time sink
Calendar and recording tools came next to support meeting prep workflows
Chad Ads integration was added when Will wanted to check campaign performance without logging into the Google Ads dashboard
Email triage and meeting review pipelines were the latest addition - moving from reactive chat to proactive processing

invariants.name: Tool output is truncated at 256KB
invariants.description: Any tool result longer than 256KB is truncated to prevent memory issues and to stay within Claude's context limits. This affects long email searches and large transcript fetches.

invariants.name: Tool calls and results are not persisted to the database
invariants.description: Only the final user messages and assistant text responses are saved to chat_messages. The intermediate tool calls and tool results exist only in memory during the request. When loading chat history for a continued conversation, tool context is lost.

entities.name: ChatThread
entities.description: A conversation between a user and Claude. Has a title (default 'New Chat') and contains ordered messages. The updated_at timestamp changes whenever a new message is added, so threads sort by most recent activity.
key_fields: user_id - who owns this thread
title - display name, defaults to 'New Chat', can be renamed
updated_at - changes on every message, used for sorting

entities.name: ChatMessage
entities.description: A single message in a chat thread. Stores the role (user or assistant) and the text content. Tool calls and their results are NOT stored here - only the final text that the user sees.
key_fields: thread_id - which thread this belongs to (cascade deletes if thread is deleted)
role - 'user' or 'assistant'
content - the full text of the message

functions.name: Chat Stream Handler
functions.description: The main POST /api/chat endpoint. Authenticates the user, loads their Google tokens, sets up the tool executor with the user's context (timezone, email, admin status), and starts the streaming loop with Claude.

decisions.name: Authentication Check
decisions.description: Verifies the auth_token from the request against the database. If invalid, returns 401 immediately. This happens before any Claude interaction.

decisions.name: User Context Injection
decisions.description: The user's local time, timezone, and email are extracted from the request and injected into the system prompt. This lets Claude know what 'today' and 'this week' mean in the user's timezone, preventing date calculation errors.
decisions.rationale: Without timezone context, Claude would use UTC or guess, leading to off-by-one day errors for users in US timezones.

functions.name: Tool Execution Loop
functions.description: After Claude responds, check if it wants to call any tools. If yes, execute each tool via the ToolExecutor, send the results back to Claude, and repeat. Continue until Claude stops requesting tools or the iteration limit is hit.

decisions.name: 50-Iteration Safety Limit
decisions.description: The loop runs a maximum of 50 iterations. If Claude keeps requesting tools after 50 rounds, the loop terminates. This prevents infinite loops where Claude gets stuck in a tool-calling cycle.
decisions.rationale: In practice, most requests complete in 1-5 iterations. The 50 limit is a safety net, not a normal operating bound.

decisions.name: 5-Minute Tool Timeout
decisions.description: Each individual tool execution has a 5-minute timeout. If a tool (like a large email search or workspace-wide scan) takes longer than 5 minutes, it's killed. The overall chat endpoint has a 300-second timeout.
decisions.rationale: Some admin tools (search all workspace emails) can be slow when scanning many accounts. 5 minutes is generous but prevents hung connections.

decisions.name: Sequential Tool Execution
decisions.description: Tools are executed one at a time, not in parallel. Even if Claude requests multiple tools in one response, they run sequentially. This maintains consistent state - one tool's side effects are visible to the next.
decisions.rationale: Parallel execution would create race conditions, especially for tools that modify state (email sending, draft saving).

functions.name: Tool Executor
functions.description: Routes tool calls to the correct handler based on the tool name. Holds references to all service clients (Gmail, Calendar, Recorder, Chad Ads) and the user's context. Handles scope checking before executing tools that need specific OAuth permissions.

decisions.name: OAuth Scope Verification
decisions.description: Before executing Gmail or Calendar tools, checks that the user has granted the required OAuth scopes. If scopes are missing, returns an error message telling Claude to ask the user to authorize. This prevents cryptic 403 errors from Google.

decisions.name: Admin Tool Guard
decisions.description: Admin-only tools (search other users' email, list workspace users, search all workspace calendars) check the is_admin flag before executing. Non-admin users get a clear error message.

functions.name: Claude API Client
functions.description: Sends messages to the Anthropic API and streams the response. Constructs the message array with system prompt, user messages, and tool results. Defines all 30+ available tools in the API request.

decisions.name: System Prompt Construction
decisions.description: Builds a system prompt that includes: the user's current local time and timezone, instructions for email confirmation flow, calendar timezone handling, recording search strategy, and Chad Ads conversation management. This prompt is the primary way business rules are communicated to Claude.
decisions.rationale: Claude doesn't inherently know the business rules. The system prompt is the only mechanism to enforce behaviors like email confirmation and recording search fallback chains.

decisions.name: Conditional Tool Availability
decisions.description: Admin-only tools are only included in the API request if the user is_admin. This means Claude doesn't even know admin tools exist for non-admin users, preventing it from trying to use them.
decisions.rationale: Hiding tools entirely is more reliable than telling Claude 'don't use these' - it can't call what it doesn't know about.

functions.name: SSE Streaming
functions.description: Streams events to the frontend via Server-Sent Events. Event types: 'text' (Claude's words), 'tool_call' (which tool Claude is invoking), 'tool_result' (what the tool returned), 'tool_progress' (long-running tool updates), 'error' (tool failures), 'done' (stream complete).

decisions.name: Progress Events for Long Tools
decisions.description: Long-running tools (email searches, workspace scans) send periodic 'tool_progress' events so the user sees something happening. For email searches, progress fires every 10 emails processed.
decisions.rationale: Without progress events, the user stares at a blank screen for 30+ seconds during large searches, leading them to think it's broken.

children.name: Gmail
children.intent: Lets Claude read and send email on behalf of the user. Searching, reading threads, composing new messages, replying, and saving drafts - all through natural language in chat.
children.description: Gmail API integration with full email lifecycle: search, read, send (with confirmation), reply, reply-all, and draft. Handles RFC 2822 message construction, multi-part MIME parsing, and Gmail deep link generation. Admin users can search other team members' inboxes via delegation.
children.why: Email is the team's primary communication channel with clients. Being able to search, read, and reply to emails through chat saves constant context-switching between the chat interface and Gmail.
children.who: All team members, especially Will who manages client relationships directly. Admin delegation lets Will check team members' inboxes when covering for them.
failure_modes: Token expired and refresh fails: tool returns scope error, Claude asks user to re-authorize
Email body too large: truncated at 256KB, user may miss content at the end
Gmail API rate limited: tool returns error, Claude can retry or inform user
RFC 2822 construction fails: send/reply fails, draft can still be saved
validation: Send a test email via Claude and verify it arrives with correct formatting, recipients, and threading
Verify reply threading by replying to an email and checking it appears in the correct Gmail thread
Test admin delegation by searching another team member's inbox and verifying results match their actual emails
Check that the user_confirmed gate cannot be bypassed - ask Claude to send an email without confirmation and verify it refuses
limitations: Full email bodies are fetched for every message in search results, making large searches slow
HTML emails are converted to plain text, which sometimes loses formatting and makes the content hard to read
No attachment support - Claude can't view or send attachments
history: First integration built after Claude chat was working
Reply and reply-all were added after users kept asking Claude to respond to emails
Admin delegation was added so Will could search team members' inboxes during coverage

invariants.name: Email sends always require explicit user confirmation
invariants.description: Claude must draft the email in chat, show the recipients, subject, and body to the user, and wait for explicit approval before sending. The user_confirmed flag can only be set by Claude after the user says yes. This prevents accidental email sends.

invariants.name: OAuth tokens are refreshed proactively before every API call
invariants.description: Before making any Gmail, Calendar, or other Google API call, the system refreshes the access token proactively. If the refresh fails, it falls back to the cached token. There is no retry-on-401 logic - the refresh happens before the call, not after a failure.

functions.name: Email Search
functions.description: Searches the user's Gmail using Gmail's native query syntax. Returns parsed emails with sender, subject, snippet, date, and thread ID. Supports pagination and sends progress updates every 10 emails for large searches.

decisions.name: Progress Every 10 Emails
decisions.description: During large searches, sends a tool_progress event to the frontend every 10 emails processed. This keeps the user informed during searches that might take 10-30 seconds.

decisions.name: 500 Email Result Cap
decisions.description: Search results are capped at 500 emails. Combined with the 256KB output truncation, this prevents a broad search from overwhelming Claude's context window.

functions.name: Email Reading
functions.description: Fetches a single email or an entire thread with full message bodies. Parses multi-part MIME to extract readable text, preferring text/plain over HTML. Generates Gmail deep links using the user's email as the authuser parameter so links open in the correct account.

decisions.name: Text/Plain Preferred Over HTML
decisions.description: When parsing email bodies, prefers text/plain parts over HTML. Falls back to HTML if no plain text is available. This gives Claude clean text to work with instead of HTML markup.

decisions.name: Multi-Account Gmail Deep Links
decisions.description: Gmail links include ?authuser=EMAIL so they open in the correct Google account when the user has multiple accounts signed in. Format: https://mail.google.com/mail/?authuser=EMAIL#inbox/THREAD_ID

functions.name: Email Sending
functions.description: Composes and sends a new email. Requires the user_confirmed flag to be true - Claude must show the draft to the user and get explicit approval before this flag is set. Constructs RFC 2822 format with proper headers.

decisions.name: User Confirmation Gate
decisions.description: The send_email tool has a user_confirmed parameter. Claude must set this to true only after the user has explicitly approved the email. If user_confirmed is false or missing, the tool returns an error telling Claude to show the draft and ask for confirmation first.
decisions.rationale: This is the primary safety mechanism against accidental email sends. Claude drafts, user reviews, user confirms, then Claude sends.

decisions.name: RFC 2822 Message Construction
decisions.description: Builds the raw email message with From, To, CC, BCC, Subject, and body headers in RFC 2822 format. The message is base64url-encoded before sending to the Gmail API. Helper function handles the format so individual tools don't need to know the spec.

functions.name: Email Replying
functions.description: Replies to an existing email within a thread. Extracts the original message's headers (In-Reply-To, References, Message-ID) to maintain proper threading. Prepends 'Re: ' to the subject if not already present. Supports both reply (to sender only) and reply-all (to all recipients).

decisions.name: Threading Header Extraction
decisions.description: Fetches the original email to extract its Message-ID, In-Reply-To, and References headers. Sets In-Reply-To to the original Message-ID and appends it to References. This ensures the reply appears in the correct thread in all email clients.
decisions.rationale: Without proper threading headers, replies show up as new conversations instead of being grouped with the original thread.

functions.name: Draft Saving
functions.description: Saves an email as a draft without sending it. Uses the Gmail /drafts endpoint. Supports saving reply drafts with proper threading headers (in_reply_to, references parameters).

functions.name: Admin Email Search (Delegation)
functions.description: Allows admin users to search other team members' Gmail accounts. Uses Google service account delegation to impersonate the target user. Can search a specific user's email or scan all workspace users in parallel.

decisions.name: Parallel Workspace Search
decisions.description: When searching all workspace emails, spawns concurrent tasks (via JoinSet) for each team member's inbox. Results are aggregated and sorted. Progress events fire as each user's search completes.
decisions.rationale: Sequential search across 6 users would take 6x as long. Parallel search reduces wall-clock time to roughly the slowest individual search.

children.name: Calendar
children.intent: Lets Claude look up the user's schedule, find events, check attendees, and answer questions about meetings. Read-only access to Google Calendar with timezone-aware date handling.
children.description: Google Calendar API integration for reading events across all of a user's calendars. Handles timezone conversion, multi-calendar aggregation, deduplication, and attendee details. Admin users can query other team members' calendars via delegation.
children.why: Meeting prep is a big part of the team's workflow - knowing who you're meeting with, what was discussed last time, and what's coming up. Calendar access through chat makes this information instantly available.
children.who: All team members for checking their schedules. Will uses it heavily before client calls to review upcoming meetings and attendee context.
failure_modes: Timezone extraction fails: defaults to UTC (+00:00), dates may be off by hours
One calendar errors: that calendar is skipped, events from other calendars still return
Event deduplication by iCalUID: same event with different UIDs shows as duplicates
validation: Query events for today and verify they match what's actually on the calendar
Test timezone handling by querying events at day boundaries (11pm, midnight, 1am) and checking the right day's events return
Verify multi-calendar aggregation by creating an event on a shared calendar and confirming it appears in search results
limitations: Read-only access - Claude cannot create, modify, or delete calendar events
All-day events show as date strings without time, which can be confusing
Multi-calendar deduplication works by event ID, but shared events sometimes appear as duplicates with different IDs
history: Added after Gmail to support meeting prep workflows
Multi-calendar aggregation was added when team members complained about missing events on shared calendars
Timezone handling was a recurring source of bugs - the current proactive approach (extracting offset from user's local time) was the third attempt

invariants.name: All date logic uses the user's local timezone
invariants.description: The user's local time and IANA timezone (e.g. America/New_York) are sent with every chat request. All date calculations, calendar queries, and time-related tool calls use the user's timezone, not the server's. This prevents off-by-one day errors when the server is in a different timezone.

functions.name: List Calendars
functions.description: Shows all calendars the user has access to, categorized by visibility: visible (checked in the UI), unchecked (accessible but not shown), and hidden. Useful for Claude to understand which calendars to search.

functions.name: Get Calendar Events
functions.description: Searches for events within a date range across ALL of the user's calendars. Deduplicates events that appear in multiple calendars, sorts by start time, and returns details including attendees, location, Meet links, and description.

decisions.name: Multi-Calendar Aggregation
decisions.description: Iterates through every calendar the user has access to, queries each one, deduplicates by event ID, and returns a unified sorted list. The user doesn't have to specify which calendar - we search all of them.
decisions.rationale: Users often have events on multiple calendars (personal, work, shared team calendars). Searching just the primary calendar would miss meetings.

decisions.name: Timezone-Aware Date Range
decisions.description: Parses the user's local time to extract their timezone offset (e.g. -04:00). Converts date ranges to RFC3339 with the user's offset. Increments the end date by 1 day to make the range inclusive (the Calendar API uses exclusive upper bounds). Sends the timeZone parameter so results come back in the user's local time.
decisions.rationale: The Calendar API is strict about RFC3339 format and uses exclusive end dates. Without proper timezone handling, a query for 'events on April 12' would either miss events or include April 13 events depending on the user's timezone.

functions.name: Get Single Event
functions.description: Fetches a specific event by ID. Returns full details including all attendees with their response status (accepted, declined, tentative, needsAction), organizer, location, Google Meet link, and full description.

decisions.name: Meet Link Extraction
decisions.description: Extracts Google Meet links from two possible locations: the hangout_link field, or the conference_data.entry_points array (looking for the 'video' type). Some events have one, some have the other, some have both.

functions.name: Events by Participant
functions.description: Finds events where a specific person is an attendee. Searches across all calendars and filters by the participant's email address. Useful for answering 'when is my next meeting with X?'

children.name: Meeting Recorder
children.intent: Lets Claude find and read meeting recordings and transcripts. Connects calendar events to their recordings so users can ask about what was discussed in a meeting.
children.description: Integration with the agency's meeting recording service. Searches for recordings by calendar event ID, participant, or date. Fetches full transcripts with speaker identification and timestamps. Non-admin users only see recordings they participated in.
children.why: Meeting recordings and transcripts are valuable but hard to access after the fact. Being able to ask Claude 'what did we discuss in the meeting with Client X last Tuesday?' and get an answer from the transcript is a huge time saver.
children.who: Will primarily, for reviewing client meeting discussions and extracting context. Also feeds into the meeting review pipeline for automated task extraction.

config.name: SEARCH_API_KEY
config.description: API key for the recording service

failure_modes: Recording not linked to calendar event: falls back to participant search, then transcript search
Transcript still processing: no segments returned, recording skipped
Non-admin user searches recording they didn't attend: filtered out, returns no results
validation: Test the three-step fallback chain: search by event ID, then by participant, then by transcript content - verify each step finds the right recording
Verify non-admin users can only see their own recordings
Check transcript formatting - speaker labels should be correct and timestamps should match the recording
limitations: Not all meetings have recordings - only those with the recording bot enabled
Transcript quality varies with audio quality and number of speakers
Non-admin users can only see recordings they participated in, which can be limiting when covering for a colleague
The fallback search chain (event ID > participant > transcript) is necessary because recording-to-calendar linking is unreliable
history: Integrated the agency's existing recording service rather than building recording capability
The three-step fallback chain was developed through trial and error as direct event ID lookup proved unreliable
Admin access to all recordings was added for Will's oversight role

functions.name: Search by Calendar Event
functions.description: The most precise way to find a recording - looks up recordings linked to a specific calendar event ID. This is the first method Claude should try when looking for a meeting recording.

functions.name: Search by Participant
functions.description: Finds recordings where a specific person was an attendee. Searches by email or name. This is the fallback when no calendar event ID is available.

decisions.name: Non-Admin Participant Filtering
decisions.description: For non-admin users, search results are filtered to only show recordings where the user was an attendee. Email comparison is case-insensitive. Admin users see all recordings regardless of participation.
decisions.rationale: Users shouldn't be able to listen in on meetings they weren't part of. Admin access is the exception for oversight purposes.

functions.name: Get Full Transcript
functions.description: Fetches the complete transcript for a recording by ID. Returns segments with speaker name, text, and timestamps (formatted as HH:MM:SS). Segments may have gaps - the transcript isn't necessarily continuous.

functions.name: Search Transcript Content
functions.description: Full-text search across transcript content. Finds recordings where specific words or phrases were spoken. This is the last resort in the fallback chain when other search methods don't find the right recording.

functions.name: Recording Lookup Fallback Chain
functions.description: The system prompt instructs Claude to follow a specific order when looking for meeting recordings: 1) Search by calendar event ID (most precise), 2) Search by participant email from the calendar event, 3) Search transcript content as a last resort.

decisions.name: Three-Step Fallback Strategy
decisions.description: Step 1: If the user mentions a meeting, look up the calendar event first to get its ID, then search recordings by event ID. Step 2: If no recording found by event ID, use the attendee emails from the calendar event to search by participant. Step 3: If still no recording, search transcript content for keywords from the user's question.
decisions.rationale: Event ID is the most reliable link but not all recordings have one. Participant search is broader. Transcript search is the broadest but least precise. Trying them in order of precision minimizes false matches.

children.name: Chad Ads (Google Ads)
children.intent: Lets Claude interact with Google Ads accounts through a conversational interface. Users can check campaign performance, create campaigns, and manage ads by chatting naturally.
children.description: Integration with the Chad Ads API, which provides a chat-style interface to Google Ads. Multi-turn conversations are tracked per customer (ad account) ID. Supports both read operations (checking metrics) and write operations (creating campaigns, changing budgets) with safety checks for writes.
children.why: Will manages Google Ads campaigns for clients and was tired of logging into the Google Ads dashboard to check basic metrics. Being able to ask 'how is the Nzymes campaign performing this week?' in chat is much faster.
children.who: Will exclusively - he manages all client ad accounts. Read operations are frequent (checking performance), write operations are rare and require confirmation.

config.name: CHAD_ADS_SECRET
config.description: Admin secret for Chad Ads API authentication

dependencies: SQLite database (for token and conversation storage)
failure_modes: Conversation history exceeds 256KB: truncated, losing earlier context in long sessions
Chad Ads API down: tool returns error, Claude informs user
Customer not selected: tool returns error asking Claude to call list_customers first
Empty assistant message in history: filtered out before API call to prevent rejection
validation: Ask about campaign performance and verify the numbers match the Google Ads dashboard
Test multi-turn conversations - ask a follow-up question and verify it has context from the previous answer
Verify write safety by asking Claude to create a campaign and checking it asks for confirmation first
Test customer switching - change accounts mid-conversation and verify the history resets
limitations: Conversation history is ephemeral - stored in tool result text, not the database, so it's lost between chat sessions
The history can get truncated at 256KB if a conversation about multiple campaigns gets long
Write safety is enforced via the system prompt, not code - Claude could theoretically skip the confirmation if the prompt doesn't catch the edge case
Only supports Google Ads via the Chad Ads API - no Meta Ads integration in chat yet
history: Added as a conversational wrapper around the Chad Ads API
Multi-turn conversation tracking was needed because Google Ads queries often require follow-up questions about specific campaigns or date ranges
Auto-select for single-account users was added after Will got tired of being asked 'which account?' every time

entities.name: ChadAdsToken
entities.description: OAuth credentials for a user's Google Ads account, obtained through the Chad Ads integration. One record per user. Stores the user token and optionally a selected customer (ad account) ID.
key_fields: user_token - OAuth token for Chad Ads API
customer_id - nullable, the selected Google Ads customer account ID

functions.name: List Customer Accounts
functions.description: Shows all Google Ads customer accounts connected to the user. If exactly one account exists, it's auto-selected. If multiple exist, Claude presents the list and asks the user which one to work with.

decisions.name: Auto-Select Single Account
decisions.description: If the user has exactly one Google Ads customer account, it's automatically selected without asking. This avoids a needless 'which account?' question for users with only one account.

functions.name: Ask Chad Ads
functions.description: Sends a message to the Chad Ads API as part of a multi-turn conversation. Takes the conversation history (array of role/content messages), appends the new message, gets the response, and returns the updated history. The history is formatted with a customer_id marker so Claude knows which account it belongs to.

decisions.name: Ephemeral Conversation History
decisions.description: Conversation history is stored in the tool result text, not in the database. It's formatted as [CHAD_ADS_CONVERSATION_HISTORY:customer_id=X:JSON] so Claude can parse it and pass it back on the next message. History is lost when the chat session ends.
decisions.rationale: Storing history in the tool result keeps it simple - no extra database table needed. The tradeoff is that history is lost between chat sessions, but for ad management queries this is acceptable.
decisions.uncertainty_note: I'm not sure if the history format is robust enough - if the JSON gets very large, it could hit the 256KB truncation limit and corrupt the history.

decisions.name: Empty Message Filtering
decisions.description: Before sending to the Chad Ads API, filters out any assistant messages with empty content. This prevents 'empty assistant turn' errors from the API.

functions.name: Connection Flow
functions.description: Users connect their Google Ads account through an OAuth flow. The frontend shows a 'Connect Chad Ads' button if no token exists. Clicking it redirects to the Chad Ads OAuth page. After authorization, the callback stores the token and auto-selects the customer account if there's only one.

decisions.name: Auth Token Verification in Callback
decisions.description: The Chad Ads OAuth callback verifies the user's auth token (passed in the state parameter) before storing the Chad Ads token. This prevents token injection - someone can't forge a callback to link their ads account to another user.

functions.name: Write Operation Safety
functions.description: The system prompt instructs Claude to ask for user confirmation before performing write operations in Google Ads (creating campaigns, changing budgets, enabling/pausing ads). Read operations (checking metrics, listing campaigns) don't need confirmation.

decisions.name: Prompt-Level Write Safety
decisions.description: Write safety for Chad Ads is enforced via the system prompt, not via code. Claude is told to ask before writes. This is softer than the email confirmation gate (which has a code-level user_confirmed flag), but sufficient because Chad Ads writes go through their own API which has its own undo mechanisms.
decisions.rationale: Unlike email (which can't be unsent), most Google Ads changes can be reversed. Prompt-level enforcement provides a reasonable safety net without the complexity of a code-level gate.

children.name: Google Tasks
children.intent: Lets Claude and the triage UI create, manage, and organize tasks in Google Tasks. Used both directly through chat and as the backend for meeting review task creation.
children.why: The meeting review pipeline needed a way to turn extracted action items into actual trackable tasks. Google Tasks was chosen because the team already uses it and it integrates with Google Calendar.
children.who: Used indirectly through the meeting review pipeline (auto-creating tasks from meeting action items) and directly through chat (Claude can create/manage tasks on request).
dependencies: Service account (for admin delegation to other users' task lists)
failure_modes: Task list doesn't exist: creation fails with clear error
Delegation fails for workspace user: falls back to creating task in current user's account
Duplicate task detection: skips if same title already exists in the list
validation: Create a task via Claude and verify it appears in Google Tasks with correct title, notes, and due date
Test the meeting review approval flow end-to-end: approve a draft task and verify it appears in the assignee's Google Tasks
Verify deduplication by approving the same task twice - it should only create one Google Task
limitations: Google Tasks has a flat hierarchy - no sub-tasks beyond one level of nesting
No due date reminders through the chat interface - users have to check their task lists
Task list organization is per-user, so there's no shared team task list
history: Added specifically to support the meeting review pipeline's task creation
Chat tools were added afterwards so team members could manage tasks directly through conversation

entities.name: GoogleTask
entities.description: A task in Google Tasks. Represents an action item that can be created, updated, completed, or deleted through the API.
key_fields: title - task description
notes - additional details or context
due_date - when the task is due (nullable)
status - needsAction or completed
parent_task - ID of parent task for subtasks (nullable)
task_list_id - which task list this belongs to

entities.name: GoogleTaskList
entities.description: A task list in Google Tasks. Contains multiple tasks and can be created, renamed, or deleted.
key_fields: title - name of the task list
id - Google's unique identifier for the list

functions.name: Tasks API Client
functions.description: HTTP client for Google Tasks API. Supports CRUD for both task lists and individual tasks. Handles pagination for large lists.

functions.name: Tasks Chat Tools
functions.description: 11 tool functions available to Claude: list/create/update/delete task lists, list/get/create/update/complete/delete/move tasks. Formats output with checkbox icons, due dates, and direct links. Admin variants allow managing other users' tasks.

children.name: Stripe
children.intent: Admin-only tools for querying Stripe billing data. Lets Claude look up customers, subscriptions, invoices, charges, and disputes for financial reporting and client management.
children.why: Will needed to quickly check billing status for clients without logging into the Stripe dashboard. Questions like 'has Client X paid this month?' or 'what's our total MRR?' come up in client management.
children.who: Will only - all Stripe tools are admin-gated. Used for financial reporting and checking client payment status.

config.name: STRIPE_API_KEY
config.description: Stripe secret key for billing queries

failure_modes: Invalid customer ID: Stripe returns 404, tool shows clear error
Date range parsing fails: falls back to no date filter, returns all results
API rate limited: tool returns error
validation: Query invoices and verify amounts match the Stripe dashboard
Test currency formatting with a non-USD invoice to verify zero-decimal currency handling
Verify admin-only access by attempting Stripe tools as a non-admin user - should be rejected
limitations: Read-only - cannot create charges, refund payments, or modify subscriptions through chat
No revenue aggregation or MRR calculation built in - Claude has to do the math from raw invoice/subscription data
Stripe API rate limits apply - large queries across many customers can be slow
history: Added for financial visibility during client management conversations
Admin-only restriction was a deliberate choice - billing data is sensitive and only Will needs access

invariants.name: Stripe tools are admin-only
invariants.description: All Stripe tools require is_admin=true. Non-admin users cannot access any billing data through chat.

functions.name: Stripe Chat Tools
functions.description: 5 admin-only tool functions: list customers, list subscriptions, list invoices, list charges, list disputes. All support filtering by date range, customer, status. Human-readable formatting with currency conversion.

systems.name: Authentication
systems.intent: Manages who can use the system and what permissions they have. Handles the entire Google OAuth lifecycle from initial login through token refresh and scope management.
systems.description: Google OAuth flow with scope tracking, token refresh, admin detection, and session management. Users log in with Google, get a session token stored in localStorage, and their OAuth tokens are refreshed proactively before every API call.
systems.why: Everyone on the team already has Google Workspace accounts, so OAuth was the natural choice - no separate passwords to manage, and it gives us access to Gmail and Calendar APIs automatically.
systems.who: All team members authenticate through their @digitalwillads.com Google accounts. Will is the primary admin.

config.name: GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID
config.description: OAuth client ID from Google Cloud Console

config.name: SERVICE_ACCOUNT_PATH
config.description: Path to service account JSON for admin delegation (default: service_account.json)

failure_modes: Token refresh fails: falls back to cached access token (may still work if not expired)
Directory API fails: user treated as non-admin, login continues normally
Service account JSON missing: admin delegation tools fail, regular tools still work
Refresh token revoked by user: all API calls fail until user re-authenticates
validation: After login, verify the user record was updated (email, name, picture, admin status)
Test incremental scope authorization - grant gmail.readonly first, then add gmail.send later, verify both work
Test admin detection with a known admin account and a known non-admin account
Verify token refresh by waiting for the access token to expire (~1 hour) and confirming the next API call succeeds
limitations: Admin detection is best-effort via Directory API - if that call fails, the user is treated as non-admin until next login
Auth tokens never expire server-side, so old sessions accumulate in the database
If a user revokes Google access, the stored refresh token becomes invalid but we don't detect this until the next API call fails
history: Initially used a simple password system, switched to Google OAuth when we needed Gmail API access anyway
Added incremental scope authorization when gmail.send was added - users don't need to re-authorize everything just to grant send access
Admin detection was added to gate workspace-wide search tools

invariants.name: Google OAuth is the only auth method
invariants.description: There is no password login. Every user authenticates through Google OAuth with their @digitalwillads.com account. If a user isn't logged in through Google, they can't use the system at all.

invariants.name: Admin users can access other team members' data
invariants.description: Users with is_admin=true can search other team members' Gmail, Calendar, and see all meeting recordings. Non-admin users can only access their own data. Admin status is detected via Google Directory API on login (best-effort - failures don't block login).

entities.name: User
entities.description: A person who uses the chat interface. Has a Google account, authenticates via OAuth, and owns chat threads. Can be an admin (detected via Google Directory API) which grants access to other team members' data.
key_fields: google_sub - Google's unique user identifier, used as the primary key for upserts
email - their @digitalwillads.com email address
name - display name from Google profile
picture_url - Google profile photo URL (nullable)
is_admin - whether they have admin privileges (0 or 1, detected on login)

entities.name: AuthToken
entities.description: A session token stored in the user's browser localStorage. Created on login, used to authenticate every chat request. Has no server-side expiration - the app controls when to forget it.
key_fields: token - UUID string, unique per session
user_id - which user this session belongs to
expires_at - nullable, not enforced server-side

entities.name: GoogleTokens
entities.description: OAuth credentials for a user's Google account. One record per user (unique on user_id). Contains both the short-lived access token and the long-lived refresh token. Scopes track what permissions the user has granted.
key_fields: access_token - short-lived token for Google API calls, refreshed proactively
refresh_token - long-lived token used to get new access tokens, never expires if offline access was granted
scopes - space-separated string of granted OAuth scopes (e.g. 'gmail.readonly gmail.send calendar.readonly')
token_expiry - nullable, not enforced (refresh happens proactively, not on expiry)

functions.name: Login Flow
functions.description: User clicks 'Sign in with Google', gets redirected to Google consent screen with requested scopes, Google redirects back with an auth code, we exchange it for tokens, fetch the user's profile, detect admin status, create/update the user record, and issue a session token.

decisions.name: Admin Detection via Directory API
decisions.description: After login, calls the Google Directory API to check if the user is an admin. This is best-effort - if the API call fails, the user is treated as non-admin. Admin status is stored on the user record and used to gate admin-only tools.
decisions.rationale: We use the Directory API rather than a hardcoded list so that admin changes in Google Workspace are automatically reflected. The best-effort approach ensures login isn't blocked by transient API failures.

decisions.name: User Upsert on Every Login
decisions.description: Every time a user logs in, their user record is created or updated using google_sub as the unique key. Email, name, picture, and admin status are all refreshed. This means profile changes in Google are picked up automatically.
decisions.rationale: People change their names and profile photos. Upserting on login keeps our records current without a sync job.

functions.name: Token Refresh
functions.description: Called before every Gmail/Calendar API call. Uses the stored refresh_token to get a fresh access_token from Google. If the refresh fails, falls back to the cached access_token (which might still be valid).

decisions.name: Proactive Refresh Strategy
decisions.description: Tokens are refreshed BEFORE making API calls, not after getting a 401. This means every tool execution starts with a fresh token. If the refresh fails, the existing cached token is used as a fallback.
decisions.rationale: Proactive refresh avoids the complexity of retry-on-401 logic and reduces latency (no failed request + retry cycle). The fallback covers the rare case where refresh fails but the cached token is still valid.

functions.name: Scope Management
functions.description: Tracks which OAuth scopes the user has granted. If a tool needs a scope the user hasn't granted (e.g. gmail.send), shows a banner asking them to authorize. Uses Google's incremental authorization to add scopes without revoking existing ones.

decisions.name: Incremental Authorization
decisions.description: When requesting new scopes, uses include_granted_scopes=true so Google merges the new scope with existing grants. The user only sees a consent screen for the new scope, not all scopes. After authorization, the stored scopes string is updated.
decisions.rationale: Without incremental auth, adding gmail.send would revoke the user's existing gmail.readonly grant, breaking things.

functions.name: Session Management
functions.description: Auth tokens (UUIDs) are stored in the auth_tokens table and in the user's browser localStorage. Every API request includes the token for verification. Tokens have no server-side expiration.

decisions.name: No Server-Side Token Expiration
decisions.description: Auth tokens don't expire on the server. The browser controls the session lifetime. A user stays logged in until they clear localStorage or the token is deleted from the database.
decisions.rationale: This is a team-internal tool, not a public-facing app. The convenience of persistent sessions outweighs the security tradeoff for this use case.
decisions.uncertainty_note: I'm not sure if there's any cleanup mechanism for old auth tokens, or if they accumulate forever in the database.

systems.name: Email Triage
systems.intent: Automatically processes incoming Gmail messages, filters noise using AI classification, generates summaries, and presents relevant emails for human triage through a dedicated UI tab.
systems.why: The team was spending 1-2 hours per day just reading and sorting email. Most of it was noise - receipts, calendar invites, newsletters, automated notifications. They were missing important client emails buried in the noise. The triage pipeline filters out the junk and surfaces what matters.
systems.who: Will primarily, though all team members have it available. Will reviews 30-50 emails per day through the triage queue instead of scrolling through 200+ raw inbox messages.

config.name: ANTHROPIC_API_KEY
config.description: Claude API key for AI classification and summarization

dependencies: Anthropic Claude API (for classification and summarization)
failure_modes: AI classification fails: email is KEPT as fallback (safe default - better to show a noisy email than hide an important one)
AI summarization fails: email stored without summary, user sees raw snippet instead
Calendar context fails: email processed without meeting context, summary may be less contextual
Gmail API rate limited: poll cycle logs error and retries next cycle
Polling task crashes: restarts on next server boot, emails queue up in Gmail meanwhile
validation: Review the filter_reason field on filtered emails - are the AI's reasons sensible? Check for false positives (important emails filtered) and false negatives (noise getting through)
Compare AI summaries against actual email content - are they accurate and actionable?
Check that calendar context is being injected by looking for 'Meeting:' references in summaries for emails from people you've met with recently
Monitor the pending email count - if it's growing faster than you can triage, the filter isn't aggressive enough
Spot-check filtered emails weekly to catch systematic classification errors (e.g. filtering out a new client's domain)
limitations: AI classification isn't perfect - occasionally filters out an important email or lets noise through
Email polling is every 120 seconds, so there's up to a 2-minute delay. Will be replaced by Gmail webhooks once live for near real-time delivery
Calendar context enrichment only looks back 30 days, so context from older client relationships is missed
No ability to create filter rules through the UI yet - filter rules have to be added directly to the database
history: Built because Will said he was 'drowning in email' and wanted Claude to pre-sort his inbox
The AI classification layer was added after simple keyword/domain filtering wasn't selective enough
Calendar context enrichment was added so Claude could say 'you met with this person last week about X' when triaging their email
Planning to replace 120-second polling with Gmail push notifications (webhooks) once live - will make email appear in the triage queue in near real-time instead of up to 2 minutes delay

invariants.name: Emails are filtered before users see them
invariants.description: The pipeline applies user-defined filter rules (sender/domain/keyword blacklists) AND an AI classification step. Emails classified as noise (receipts, billing, newsletters, calendar invites, automated notifications) are filtered out. Only emails the AI classifies as 'KEEP' appear in the triage queue.

invariants.name: Email polling runs every 120 seconds
invariants.description: A background task runs continuously, polling Gmail for all users with OAuth tokens. Each poll fetches new messages since the last known history ID.

entities.name: EmailQueueItem
entities.description: A single email that has been processed by the triage pipeline. Tracks whether it was filtered out, why, and includes an AI-generated summary for quick scanning.
key_fields: gmail_message_id - Gmail's unique message identifier
gmail_thread_id - Gmail's thread identifier for grouping
user_id - which user this email belongs to
sender - email sender address
subject - email subject line
snippet - Gmail's preview snippet
status - queue status (pending, triaged, archived)
filtered_out - boolean, whether AI/rules filtered this email
filter_reason - why it was filtered (nullable)
ai_summary - 1-2 sentence AI-generated summary
raw_email_json - full email payload for chat context

entities.name: EmailFilterRule
entities.description: A user-defined rule for filtering emails before AI classification. Rules can match on sender, domain, or keywords.
key_fields: user_id - which user owns this rule
rule_type - type of match (sender, domain, keyword)
value - the value to match against
action - what to do when matched (filter, keep)

entities.name: EmailWebhookState
entities.description: Tracks the polling state for each user's Gmail account. Stores the last history ID so we only fetch new messages on each poll.
key_fields: user_id - which user this state belongs to
history_id - Gmail's history ID, used to fetch only new messages
watch_expiration - when the Gmail push notification watch expires (nullable)
last_poll_at - timestamp of the last successful poll

functions.name: Email Polling Loop
functions.description: Runs every 120s, discovers all users with Google tokens, fetches new Gmail messages since last history ID, feeds each through the pipeline.

functions.name: Email Processing Pipeline
functions.description: Core processing: applies filter rules, calls Claude to classify (KEEP vs FILTER with reason), generates 1-2 sentence AI summary, enriches with calendar context (recent meetings with sender in last 30 days), stores result in email_queue.

decisions.name: AI Classification
decisions.description: Claude decides keep vs filter, filtering out receipts/billing/newsletters/calendar invites/automated emails/sales pitches.

decisions.name: Calendar Context Enrichment
decisions.description: Looks back 30 days for meetings with the sender to give context about the relationship and recent interactions.

functions.name: Email Chat Endpoint
functions.description: POST /api/email-chat. Lets the user have a conversation about a specific email. Claude has access to Gmail tools and can compose replies or drafts. Uses reply_to_email (not search) since the email ID is already known.

functions.name: Email Triage Card
functions.description: UI component in the Emails tab. Shows queue of pending emails with AI summaries. User can read full email, chat with Claude about it, then click 'Triage Now' to move to next. Auto-advances through queue.

systems.name: Meeting Review
systems.intent: Processes meeting recordings, uses an AI agent ('Barbara Ann') to extract action items from transcripts, and presents meetings with their extracted tasks for human review and approval.
systems.why: After client meetings, action items would get discussed and agreed upon but never tracked. Tasks fell through the cracks because nobody was writing them down consistently. This pipeline automatically extracts tasks from meeting transcripts so nothing gets lost.
systems.who: Will uses this after every client meeting. The goal is for account managers (Eddie, Reileigh) to use it for their client meetings too.

config.name: SEARCH_API_KEY
config.description: API key for the meeting recording service

config.name: ANTHROPIC_API_KEY
config.description: Claude API key for Barbara Ann task extraction

dependencies: Gmail API (Barbara Ann can search emails for context)
Google Tasks API (for creating approved tasks)
failure_modes: Transcript empty: recording skipped entirely (no point extracting tasks from silence)
Barbara Ann fails to produce valid JSON: meeting stored without draft tasks, user can still review transcript and create tasks manually
Barbara Ann assigns wrong person: draft tasks are editable before approval - user can correct assignee
Google Tasks creation fails on approval: task marked as approved in DB but google_task_id is null, can retry later
Delegation fails for assignee: falls back to creating task in current user's account with a note
validation: Review extracted tasks against meeting transcripts - did Barbara Ann catch all the action items? Did she miss any?
Check assignee accuracy - are the suggested assignees the right people based on who was speaking?
Verify tasks appear in Google Tasks after approval - check the 'Meeting Action Items' list in each assignee's account
Monitor the ratio of approved vs rejected tasks - a high rejection rate means Barbara Ann's extraction needs prompt tuning
Test with a meeting where no action items were discussed - Barbara Ann should return an empty array, not hallucinate tasks
limitations: Barbara Ann (the AI agent) sometimes misidentifies who should own a task, especially when multiple people discuss an action item
Transcript quality depends on the recording service - poor audio leads to poor task extraction
Meeting polling is every 300 seconds, adding delay on top of transcript processing time. Will be replaced by webhooks once live for immediate processing when recordings are ready
No way to manually trigger processing for a specific meeting - have to wait for the polling cycle
history: Started as a manual workflow - Will would paste meeting transcripts into chat and ask Claude to extract tasks
Automated the pipeline when the manual process proved valuable but tedious
Named the extraction agent 'Barbara Ann' as an internal codename
Added Google Tasks integration so accepted tasks go directly into the team's task management system
Planning to replace 300-second polling with recording service webhooks once live - will trigger processing immediately when a recording is ready instead of waiting for the next poll cycle

invariants.name: Meeting polling runs every 300 seconds
invariants.description: A background task polls for new meeting recordings for all users with calendar access. Longer interval than email because meetings are less frequent.

invariants.name: Barbara Ann extracts tasks with full context
invariants.description: The AI agent that extracts tasks has access to all tools (email, calendar, recordings, tasks) so it can look up context about meeting participants and their recent communications before deciding what the action items are.

entities.name: MeetingQueueItem
entities.description: A meeting recording that has been processed by the review pipeline. Stores transcript and metadata for the review UI.
key_fields: recording_id - unique identifier from the recording service
user_id - which user this meeting belongs to
meeting_name - title of the calendar event or recording
meeting_date - when the meeting occurred
duration_seconds - length of the recording
attendees_json - JSON array of attendee names and emails
transcript_text - full transcript with speaker labels
status - review status (pending, reviewed, archived)

entities.name: MeetingDraftTask
entities.description: An action item extracted by Barbara Ann from a meeting transcript. Starts as a draft for human review, can be accepted (creating a Google Task) or rejected.
key_fields: meeting_queue_id - which meeting this task was extracted from
title - description of the action item
suggested_assignee - who Barbara Ann thinks should do this
assignee_email - email address of the suggested assignee
status - draft, accepted, rejected
google_task_id - ID of the created Google Task (nullable, set on accept)
google_tasklist_id - which task list it was created in (nullable)

functions.name: Meeting Polling Loop
functions.description: Runs every 300s, discovers users with calendar access, fetches new recordings from Recorder API, feeds each through the pipeline.

functions.name: Meeting Processing Pipeline
functions.description: Fetches recording and transcript, calls Barbara Ann (Claude with full tool access) to analyze the transcript and extract action items. Barbara Ann can search emails and calendar for context about attendees. Returns JSON array of tasks with titles, suggested assignees, and assignee emails. Stores meeting + draft tasks in database.

decisions.name: Task Extraction Strategy
decisions.description: Barbara Ann analyzes the full transcript with speaker labels, identifies actionable items, suggests assignees based on who was speaking and the discussion context.

decisions.name: Assignee Resolution
decisions.description: Tries to match attendee names to email addresses using calendar event data and Google Directory.

functions.name: Meeting Chat Endpoint
functions.description: POST /api/meeting-chat. Conversation about a specific meeting. Claude has access to all tools plus the transcript context. Can help refine task descriptions or suggest additional tasks.

functions.name: Meeting Review Card
functions.description: UI component in the Meetings tab. Shows meeting name, date, attendees, and auto-extracted draft tasks. Each task shows title and suggested assignee. User can accept (creates Google Task), reject, or refine tasks via chat. 'Done - Next Meeting' advances to next meeting.

systems.name: Database
systems.intent: Persists all application state - users, sessions, OAuth tokens, chat history, and integration credentials. The single source of truth for who's logged in, what they've said, and what services they've connected.
systems.description: SQLite database accessed via Diesel ORM with R2D2 connection pooling. 6 tables covering users, auth tokens, Google tokens, chat threads, chat messages, and Chad Ads tokens. All timestamps are UTC ISO 8601 strings. Migrations run automatically on startup.
systems.why: SQLite was chosen for simplicity - single-file database, no separate database server to manage, easy to deploy on Fly.io. The application is team-internal with low concurrent usage, so SQLite's limitations aren't a problem.
systems.who: Internal infrastructure - no team member interacts with this directly.
failure_modes: Connection pool exhausted (5 max): requests block until a connection is free or timeout
WAL checkpoint fails: database grows on disk, performance degrades gradually
Migration fails on startup: server crashes with error message, must fix migration SQL
Disk full: all writes fail, reads may still work from WAL cache
validation: Check database file size periodically - if it's growing without bound, old records aren't being cleaned up
Verify that concurrent chat requests don't deadlock by running multiple chats simultaneously
After a deploy with new migrations, verify the schema matches expectations by checking table structure
Monitor busy_timeout waits - if requests are frequently waiting 5s for locks, the pool size may need increasing
limitations: SQLite has limited concurrent write support - the WAL mode and busy timeout help but heavy parallel writes can still fail
No data backup strategy documented - the database file is on Fly.io's persistent volume
Old auth tokens and processed email/meeting records accumulate without cleanup
history: Chose SQLite + Diesel over Postgres to keep the deployment simple - one binary, one file
Added WAL mode and connection pooling when background polling tasks started causing lock contention
Schema grew from 3 tables (users, auth_tokens, google_tokens) to 9 as email triage and meeting review were added

invariants.name: All stored timestamps are UTC ISO 8601
invariants.description: Every timestamp in the database is stored as a UTC ISO 8601 string (e.g. '2026-04-12 14:30:00'). Timezone conversion happens at the API boundary, not in storage.

functions.name: User Management
functions.description: Creates or updates user records. Uses google_sub as the unique key for upserts. On every login, email, name, picture_url, and is_admin are refreshed from Google's profile data.

functions.name: Token Storage
functions.description: Stores and retrieves both auth tokens (session UUIDs) and Google OAuth tokens (access + refresh). Auth tokens can have multiple per user (one per browser session). Google tokens are one per user (unique on user_id).

decisions.name: One Google Token Record Per User
decisions.description: The google_tokens table has a unique constraint on user_id. Each login replaces the previous Google token. This means a user can only have one set of active OAuth credentials at a time.
decisions.rationale: A user only has one Google account. Multiple token records would create confusion about which one to use.

functions.name: Chat Storage
functions.description: Stores chat threads and messages. Threads belong to a user and have a title. Messages belong to a thread, have a role (user/assistant) and content. Thread's updated_at timestamp changes on every new message for sorting. Deleting a thread cascades to delete all its messages.

decisions.name: Tool Calls Not Persisted
decisions.description: Only the role and content of messages are stored. Tool calls, tool names, and tool results are not saved to the database. When loading a thread's history, there's no record of which tools were used.
decisions.rationale: Tool results can be very large (email bodies, transcripts) and change over time. Storing them would bloat the database and create stale data. The conversation history Claude sees is text-only.
decisions.uncertainty_note: This means when continuing a conversation in a new session, Claude has no memory of what tools it used previously. It can see its own text responses but not the tool context that led to them.

functions.name: Auto-Migration on Startup
functions.description: All pending database migrations run automatically when the server starts. The initial migration creates users, auth_tokens, google_tokens, chat_threads, and chat_messages. A second migration adds chad_ads_tokens.

relationships.description: When Claude decides to send an email, the tool loop calls the Gmail send tool, which enforces the user_confirmed gate before actually sending
Before executing any Gmail or Calendar tool, the executor refreshes the user's OAuth access token to ensure it's fresh
Before executing tools, the executor checks that the user has granted the required OAuth scopes and returns an error if scopes are missing
Calendar event IDs are passed to the recording search tool to find recordings linked to specific meetings
When recording search by event ID fails, the fallback chain uses attendee emails from the calendar event to search recordings by participant
Email sending requires user confirmation through the chat stream - Claude drafts the email, streams it to the user, waits for approval, then sends
Chad Ads conversations are multi-turn - the conversation history is passed through the tool loop across multiple iterations, building up context with each exchange
Every login creates or updates the user record in the database, refreshing profile data from Google
After login, both the session auth token and Google OAuth tokens are stored in the database
User messages and Claude's final text responses are saved to the database as the conversation progresses
After Chad Ads OAuth callback, the user's Chad Ads token and selected customer ID are stored in the database
The email pipeline refreshes OAuth tokens before fetching Gmail messages for each user
Pipeline enriches emails with calendar context by looking up recent meetings with the email sender
The email chat endpoint gives Claude access to reply tools so users can compose responses during triage
Pipeline fetches the full meeting transcript to pass to Barbara Ann for task extraction
Pipeline looks up calendar event details for attendee information
When users accept a draft task, the UI creates it in Google Tasks via the Tasks API
The meeting chat endpoint gives Claude access to task management tools for creating and organizing extracted tasks
